headline: Who Picks the Judges — After the Reforms
context: The Judicial Appointments Commission was established in 2009 to depoliticise appointments, but the PM retains final authority under Article 122B.

cards.big: The JAC recommends. The PM decides. The gap is the system.
cards.sub: In 2024, two JAC recommendations were reportedly not followed. No explanation published.

cards.big: Article 122B: PM 'consults' the Conference of Rulers.
cards.sub: Consult does not mean consent. The Rulers have never publicly rejected a nomination.

cards.big: JAC proceedings are confidential by statute.
cards.sub: No shortlists. No interview records. No published reasons for selection or rejection.

cards.big: Post-2018 reforms added civil society members to the JAC.
cards.sub: Four of nine members are still judges appointed by the same process they now oversee.

cards.big: An opaque process cannot prove it is fair — even when it is.
cards.sub: Opacity protects both integrity and interference equally.

cards.big: The JAC improved the process. Transparency would complete the reform. Resistance to transparency answers the question it raises.
